title: Winter spice simmer pot
subtitle: Cinnamon, cloves, star anise, and orange simmered on the hob
excerpt: A hob simmer pot for winter fragrance: cinnamon sticks, cloves, star anise, and fresh orange in water. Gently simmer over a low flame; the steam carries the warm spice fragrance through the house without synthetic additives or a diffuser device. Makes the house smell like Christmas regardless of what you're actually doing.
sourceNotes: Traditional simmer pot practice; no proprietary source required.

content.text: A winter spice simmer pot. The way it works is simple. Scented oils in the spices, peel, and herbs lift with the steam as it rises, carrying the fragrance through the room. Cinnamon and cloves give a warm, sweet base. Star anise adds a liquorice note that deepens the blend. Orange peel adds a bright top note. Fresh rosemary adds a clean herbal edge so the blend doesn't get too sweet.

Keep the pan on the lowest heat that maintains a gentle simmer; visible steam rising slowly, not a rolling boil. A boil burns off the fragrance compounds quickly and fills the room with too much moisture. Top up with water whenever the level drops below 2 cm; the pan must never boil dry. Do not leave the simmer pot unattended on the hob.

items.name: Cinnamon stick
items.prepNote: whole cinnamon sticks. Break into pieces to expose more surface area and release more fragrance.

items.name: Cloves
items.prepNote: whole cloves. Bruise lightly with the back of a spoon to crack the surface and release the eugenol oil.

items.name: Star anise
items.prepNote: whole star anise. Adds a warm, deep liquorice note to the blend.

items.name: Orange
items.prepNote: fresh orange, cut into thick rounds. The peel is where the fragrance is; cutting exposes the oil glands. Use an unwaxed orange if available.

items.name: Rosemary
items.prepNote: fresh rosemary sprigs. Bruise between the palms before adding. Adds a clean, resinous counterpoint to the sweet spices.

items.name: Lemon
items.prepNote: fresh lemon, thinly sliced. Optional for extra citrus lift. An alternative to the orange or in addition to it.

content.text: Place all the ingredients in the small saucepan and cover with water to about 5 cm depth. Set over the lowest possible heat that will bring the water to a bare simmer. Once simmering, maintain gently. Within 10-15 minutes the fragrance will begin moving through the house. Top up with water as needed; check the level every 30-45 minutes.

When finished, allow the pan to cool, then cover and refrigerate. The same ingredients can be used for up to 4 simmer sessions over 4 days. Each session will have slightly less fragrance intensity than the previous one as the volatile oils exhaust. Add a fresh cinnamon stick or a few more cloves before the third or fourth session to top up the scent.
4 days, covered, in the fridge. Discard the ingredients and the liquid after the 4th day. The spent ingredients (the dried spices especially) are safe to compost.

recipe.yieldDescription: One simmer pot (3-4 hours of fragrance)
